section.title: The Futility of Wealth
section.sectionRationale: Having warned about rash words before God, the Teacher turns to money. He watches the machinery of oppression grind on, exposes the restless hunger of the money lover, and traces wealth to its bitter ends: it robs sleep, it can be hoarded to a man's harm or lost in a bad venture, and it slips from naked hands at death. Then, without softening any of it, he lifts his eyes and names the one thing that changes everything, the ability to enjoy God's gifts as gifts from God's hand.
section.thesis: Money can never satisfy the heart that loves it, and none of it survives the grave; yet the same God who exposes wealth as futile gives, to those He chooses, the power to eat, drink, and rejoice in their labor as His gift.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the oppression no one escapes and the endless hunger for more; the grievous evil of wealth that harms or vanishes and leaves us as naked as we came; and the turn to joy, the God given gift of enjoying our lot.

units.label: Oppression and the hunger for more

verses.text: If you see the oppression of the poor and the denial of justice and righteousness in the province, do not be astonished at the matter; for one official is watched by a superior, and others higher still are over them.
The produce of the earth is taken by all; the king himself profits from the fields.
He who loves money is never satisfied by money, and he who loves wealth is never satisfied by income. This too is {{hevel|futile}}.
When good things increase, so do those who consume them; what then is the {{yitron|profit}} to the owner, except to behold them with his eyes?
The sleep of the worker is sweet, whether he eats little or much, but the abundance of the rich man permits him no sleep.

units.label: Wealth that harms and vanishes
units.note: naked we came, naked we go

verses.text: There is a grievous evil I have seen under the sun: wealth hoarded to the harm of its owner,
or wealth lost in a failed venture, so when that man has a son there is nothing to pass on.
As a man came from his mother’s womb, so he will depart again, naked as he arrived. He takes nothing for his labor to carry in his hands.
This too is a grievous affliction: Exactly as a man is born, so he will depart. What does he {{yitron|gain}} as he {{amal|toils}} for the wind?
Moreover, all his days he eats in darkness, with much sorrow, sickness, and anger.

units.label: The gift of enjoyment
units.note: receive your lot from God's hand

verses.text: Here is what I have seen to be good and fitting: to eat and drink, and to find satisfaction in all the labor one does under the sun during the few days of life that God has given him, for this is his lot.
Furthermore, God has given riches and wealth to every man, and He has enabled him to enjoy them, to accept his lot, and to rejoice in his labor. This is a gift from God.
For a man seldom considers the days of his life, because God keeps him occupied with the joy of his heart.

hevel.gloss: Vapor, breath, mist, the word that drums through Ecclesiastes as 'vanity' or 'futile.' It does not mean worthless so much as fleeting, insubstantial, impossible to grasp or hold. Money loved for its own sake is hevel: you reach for it and close your hand on breath.

amal.gloss: Toil, labor, the wearying work by which we make a living, often with a note of trouble or grievance. To toil for the wind is to pour out a lifetime of effort and end with empty hands.

yitron.gloss: Gain, profit, advantage, what is left over when the accounts are settled. It is a favorite question of the Teacher: what real, lasting yitron does a man have from all his toil under the sun? Wealth adds up to no surplus you can carry past the grave.

basic.context: The Teacher looks hard at money and the world it runs. He is not naive: he sees officials preying on the poor, layer upon layer of them, and a system where everyone, up to the king, feeds off the land. Then he exposes the secret of wealth: it never satisfies the person who loves it. The more you have, the more people gather to spend it, the less you sleep, and in the end you leave the world exactly as you entered it, naked, carrying nothing. Yet he does not stop in despair. He names a genuine good: to eat, drink, and enjoy your work as a gift from God.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage is realistic wisdom, not a rule to renounce all possessions. It describes the world as it actually is 'under the sun,' where wealth cannot fill the soul or survive death. The key turn is verses 18 to 20: the same things that are futile when clutched become good when received as God's gift. The difference is not the amount of money but whether it is grasped apart from God or accepted from His hand with thanks.
basic.originalLanguages: Two Hebrew words carry the weight. Hevel, breath or vapor, is the refrain 'futile': money loved for itself is like grasping at mist. Yitron is 'gain' or 'profit,' the surplus left when the accounting is done, and the Teacher keeps asking what real gain a man has from his toil, amal, his wearying labor. The answer under the sun is: none you can keep. The only gain that lasts is the joy God gives.
basic.typology: The picture of coming naked from the womb and departing naked points ahead to the truth Paul draws out, that we brought nothing into the world and can take nothing out. The 'gift' of enjoyment given by God's hand anticipates the fuller gift of the gospel, where God gives us His Son and, with Him, all things to enjoy.
basic.christ: Wealth cannot satisfy or save; it leaves us empty handed at death. Jesus warns of the man who stored up treasure and lost his soul, and calls us to be rich toward God. He is the one who, though rich, became poor so that we through His poverty might become rich. The enjoyment of daily bread as God's gift finds its home in Him, who teaches us to pray for daily bread and gives us Himself as the bread of life.
basic.god: God stands over the whole scene. He is the giver: riches, wealth, and, more importantly, the very power to enjoy them come from His hand. He is generous, wanting His people to eat, drink, and rejoice. And He alone can quiet the restless heart, keeping a person occupied with the joy of his heart so the days do not feel like a burden.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Timothy 6 warns that the love of money is a root of evil, echoing 'naked we came.' Luke 12 tells of the rich fool who was not rich toward God. Matthew 6 says we cannot serve God and money. James 5 rebukes hoarded wealth that rots.
basic.oneStory: In a fallen world, wealth is hevel, breath that cannot fill the soul or cross the grave. But the Teacher points past the money to the Giver, and the whole Bible follows that finger to Christ, who alone satisfies and who gives us all things, including the simple joy of daily bread, as gifts to be received from a Father's hand.

context.literary: This unit sits in the middle section of Ecclesiastes, where the Teacher gathers his observations 'under the sun' into hard sayings about labor, money, and satisfaction. It moves from social observation, 5:8 to 9, to a proverb on the emptiness of loving money, 5:10 to 12, to a case study of wealth that harms or vanishes, 5:13 to 17, and finally to one of the book's recurring 'enjoyment' passages, 5:18 to 20, which functions as the resting point.
context.historical: The world in view is the ancient administrative state, with its tiers of officials each answerable to a higher one, and a king who profits from the fields. Ordinary people watched wealth accumulate at the top while justice was denied in the province. Against that backdrop the Teacher's counsel to receive one's own lot with joy is quietly countercultural.
context.canonical: The passage belongs to the Bible's wisdom tradition and speaks to the whole scriptural theme of riches. It sets up the New Testament's warnings about mammon and its call to contentment, and its 'gift of God' language anticipates the gospel logic that every good thing is received, not earned or seized.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To strip away the illusion that money can satisfy the soul or secure the future, to show that wealth loved for itself is futile and cannot cross the grave, and then to redirect the reader to the God given ability to enjoy one's daily work and provision as a gift.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Much of the passage describes how the world works: oppression, restless greed, wealth that harms or disappears. It does not prescribe the oppression, nor does it forbid wealth as such. What it prescribes, gently, is a posture: receive your lot, eat and drink with gladness, and treat it all as God's gift rather than your achievement.

debates.question: Is wealth itself condemned here, or is the danger the love of it and the inability to enjoy it apart from God?

views.label: Wealth is exposed as an evil to be renounced
views.case: The Teacher piles up the dark side: wealth breeds oppression, robs sleep, harms its owner, vanishes in a bad venture, and leaves us naked. The relentless negatives suggest riches are a trap best let go.

views.label: The danger is loving and clutching wealth, not possessing it
views.case: It is the one who 'loves money' who is never satisfied, 5:10; and in 5:19 God gives riches and wealth and the power to enjoy them as a good gift. The problem is not the thing but the grasping heart that seeks life in it apart from God.

debates.bearing: The second reading fits the whole passage better: the same wealth that is futile when clutched becomes good when received from God's hand. The cure is not poverty but repentant enjoyment, holding possessions loosely as gifts.

debates.question: What does the Teacher mean by calling things hevel?

views.label: Meaningless, an expression of despair
views.case: Rendered 'vanity' or 'meaningless,' hevel can sound like a verdict that nothing matters, and some read the book as honest pessimism about a broken world.

views.label: Fleeting and ungraspable, a diagnosis not a surrender
views.case: Hevel literally means breath or vapor. It names things as transient and impossible to hold, not necessarily worthless. This reading lets the enjoyment passages stand as sincere: life is brief and slippery, so receive its good gifts now, from God, with open hands.

debates.bearing: If hevel means 'fleeting,' the Teacher is not contradicting himself when he commends joy. He is telling the truth about a passing world and then pointing to the God who gives real, if brief, gladness within it.

study.originalLanguages: The refrain word is hevel, breath or vapor, translated 'futile': the money lover chases satisfaction and closes his hand on mist, 5:10. The Teacher keeps auditing life for yitron, gain or surplus, and finds none that survives death, 5:11, 5:16. And all our amal, our wearying toil, is spent 'for the wind' if it is spent to build a kingdom we must leave naked. Set against these emptying words is a full one in 5:19: God's mattath, His gift, the power to enjoy what we have as coming from Him.

typology.type: Coming and departing naked, carrying nothing
typology.fulfillment: The New Testament truth that we brought nothing in and take nothing out, 1 Timothy 6:7, which drives us to lay up treasure in heaven through Christ.

typology.type: Bread, drink, and labor enjoyed as God's gift
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the bread of life, who teaches us to receive daily bread from the Father and gives Himself as the food that endures to eternal life.

typology.type: Riches that cannot satisfy the soul
typology.fulfillment: The true riches offered in Christ, in whom the poverty of the cross makes His people eternally rich, 2 Corinthians 8:9.

study.christ: This passage lays bare the emptiness of wealth: it cannot fill the heart, and it cannot be carried past the grave. Jesus stands exactly here when He tells of the rich fool who tore down his barns to build bigger ones and lost his soul that very night, and when He warns that no one can serve both God and money. He is the one who, being rich, became poor so that we through His poverty might become rich, and He offers the treasure that neither moth nor rust destroys. Even the humble joy the Teacher commends, bread and drink and honest work received from God's hand, is fulfilled in Christ, who gives us the Father as our Father and Himself as the bread of life, so that we can hold our possessions loosely and rejoice in the Giver more than the gift.
study.god: God is the unnamed but decisive presence in the passage. He is sovereign over the whole system, even the tiers of officials and the profiting king. He is the Giver who bestows riches and wealth, and, more tellingly, the power to enjoy them, so that enjoyment itself is grace and not achievement. He is generous, wanting His people to eat, drink, and find satisfaction in their labor. And He is the only one who can still the restless heart, keeping a person so occupied with the joy of his heart that the brief days are not spent brooding in the dark.

crossReferences.note: Godliness with contentment is great gain; we brought nothing into the world and can take nothing out; the love of money is a root of all kinds of evil.
The parable of the rich fool, who stored up treasure for himself but was not rich toward God, the very danger Ecclesiastes exposes.
No one can serve two masters; you cannot serve both God and money.
God richly provides us with everything to enjoy, echoing the gift of enjoyment in 5:19.

study.oneStory: Under the sun, wealth is breath: it cannot satisfy the heart that loves it, and it slips from naked hands at death. But the Teacher points past the money to the God who gives, and the whole Bible follows that pointing finger to Christ, who exposes the folly of storing up treasure on earth, becomes poor to make us rich, and hands us back the ordinary joys of daily bread and honest work as gifts to be received with thanks from our Father.

quiz.q: What does the Teacher say about the person who loves money?

options.text: He is never satisfied by money or income; the craving is never filled.
options.note: Verse 10 states plainly that the money lover is never satisfied.

options.text: He tends to lose sleep because of his abundance.
options.note: True of the rich man in verse 12, but the central point is that love of money never satisfies.

options.text: He will always be punished by the king.
options.note: The passage speaks of officials and the king, but not of punishing the money lover.

options.text: He eventually finds full contentment if he earns enough.
options.note: The opposite of the text: no amount ever satisfies the one who loves it.

quiz.q: What point is made by coming from the womb and departing naked, 5:15?

options.text: We take nothing with us at death; wealth cannot cross the grave.
options.note: The naked departure shows that no gain from toil can be carried out of life.

options.text: Our labor for wealth is like toiling for the wind.
options.note: A faithful echo, verse 16, though the image itself is about leaving with empty hands.

options.text: Children inherit nothing when a venture fails.
options.note: That is verse 14's case; verse 15 is the wider truth that we all leave naked.

options.text: Wealth is evenly shared among all people.
options.note: The passage says the opposite; wealth is uneven and, in the end, unkeepable.

quiz.q: According to verses 18 to 20, what makes eating, drinking, and working good rather than futile?

options.text: Receiving them as a gift from God, with the God given power to enjoy them.
options.note: The turn is that God gives both the wealth and the ability to enjoy it as His gift.

options.text: Simply choosing to be content with one's lot in life.
options.note: Contentment is involved, but the text roots it specifically in God's gift, not mere resolve.

options.text: Working harder so there is more to enjoy.
options.note: The passage does not tie joy to greater output but to God's gift.

options.text: Giving all wealth away so none of it can harm you.
options.note: The Teacher commends enjoying God's gifts, not renouncing all possessions.

quiz.q: Is wealth itself condemned in this passage?

options.text: No; the danger is loving and clutching it, while in 5:19 riches and the power to enjoy them are called God's gift.
options.note: The problem is the grasping heart, not possession as such.

options.text: Wealth is portrayed mostly as a trap that harms its owner.
options.note: The dark side is real, 5:13 to 17, but 5:19 keeps wealth as a possible gift from God.

options.text: Only the wealth of kings and officials is condemned.
options.note: The critique is broader, aimed at the love of money in anyone.

options.text: Yes, all owning of property is treated as sin.
options.note: The text never forbids possessions; it warns against loving them and commends enjoying them as gifts.

quiz.q: How does this passage point to Christ?

options.text: It exposes wealth's emptiness and the naked grave, driving us to the true riches and daily bread found in Christ.
options.note: Jesus warns against storing treasure on earth and offers Himself as the treasure that lasts.

options.text: It teaches us to be content, which Jesus also commends.
options.note: True, but it undersells the deeper line to Christ who became poor to make us rich.

options.text: It predicts that the Messiah would be wealthy.
options.note: The passage points the other way, to the One who became poor for us.

options.text: It has no connection to Christ at all.
options.note: Its theme of true riches and received gifts runs straight to the gospel.

questions: The Teacher says the money lover is never satisfied. Where do you catch yourself believing that a little more, income, savings, security, will finally be enough?
Wealth here robs sleep and slips from naked hands at death. What are you holding so tightly that it has begun to hold you?

Verse 19 calls the power to enjoy God's gifts a gift in itself. Can you name simple things, a meal, a day's work, rest, that you could receive today as gifts from God's hand rather than take for granted?
The passage warns against clutching, not against having. Where might God be inviting you to hold your possessions more loosely and your Giver more closely?
Jesus offers treasure that death cannot touch. What would change this week if you spent yourself building that treasure instead of toiling for the wind?

translationNotes.note: The word rendered 'futile' is hevel, literally breath or vapor. It pictures money loved for itself as something you reach for and cannot hold, fleeting rather than simply worthless.
'Under the sun' is the Teacher's recurring phrase for life viewed within this world alone. 'Toils for the wind' renders amal, wearying labor, spent on what cannot be kept.

The BSB reads 'for this is his lot' where some texts use a dash; here it is set off with a comma. 'This is a gift from God' translates language that makes enjoyment itself God's grant, not the person's achievement.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Ecclesiastes 5:8-20. Verse content segments concatenated in order; poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse. Em dash in 5:18 replaced with a comma. No em or en dashes used anywhere.
